entries: A political system in which citizens choose their leaders through elections and have political rights and freedoms.
A healthy democracy requires free elections, independent courts and a free press.
A form of democracy in which citizens elect representatives to make political decisions on their behalf.
In a representative democracy, voters do not vote on every law directly.
A system in which citizens vote directly on laws or major decisions, often through referendums.
Referendums are often presented as a tool of direct democracy.
A state in which power is held by elected representatives and not by a hereditary monarch.
The United States is a federal republic.
A political system in which a king or queen is the head of state, usually by inheritance.
The United Kingdom is a constitutional monarchy.
A monarchy in which the monarch’s powers are limited by a constitution and elected institutions.
In a constitutional monarchy, the monarch usually has a symbolic role.
A system in which the government depends on the support of parliament to remain in power.
In a parliamentary system, the prime minister usually leads the majority in parliament.
A system in which the president is both head of state and head of government, with powers separate from the legislature.
The American political system is often described as a presidential system.
A system in which power is divided between a central government and regional governments.
Federal systems can give states or regions significant autonomy.
A state in which political authority is mainly held by the central government.
France is often described as a unitary state, despite decentralisation.
A form of government, especially one considered in terms of how power is organised or exercised.
The article describes how the regime tightened its control over the media.
A political system in which power is concentrated in the hands of a leader or ruling group and political freedoms are limited.
Authoritarianism often weakens independent institutions and civil liberties.
A system in which one person or a small group holds absolute political power, usually without free elections.
The dictatorship banned opposition parties and censored the press.
A system in which one ruler has unlimited power and is not effectively controlled by laws or institutions.
Critics argue that the country has gradually moved towards autocracy.
A system in which religious authorities or religious law play the central role in government.
In a theocracy, political legitimacy is often based on religious authority.
A system in which a small wealthy or powerful group controls political power.
Some observers fear that economic inequality can turn a democracy into an oligarchy.
The supreme authority of a state to govern itself and make its own decisions.
Brexit supporters often argued that leaving the EU would restore national sovereignty.
The belief that a government, institution or leader has the right to rule or make decisions.
A government may lose legitimacy if it ignores the rule of law.
The official power to give orders, make decisions or enforce rules.
The crisis challenged the authority of the central government.
The way a country, institution or organisation is managed and controlled.
Good governance requires transparency, accountability and competence.
The division of political power among different branches of government, usually the executive, legislature and judiciary.
The separation of powers is meant to prevent one branch from becoming too powerful.
contre-pouvoirs ; système de freins et contrepoids
A system in which each branch of government can limit the power of the others.
Checks and balances are central to the American Constitution.
État de droit ; primauté du droit
The principle that everyone, including leaders and governments, must obey the law.
Independent courts are essential to uphold the rule of law.
A set of fundamental rules that define how a state is organised and how power is limited.
A constitution can protect citizens from abuses of power.
The part of government responsible for implementing laws and running the state.
In the United States, the president leads the executive branch.
The institution or body that makes laws, such as a parliament or congress.
The legislature debated the new immigration bill for several weeks.
The system of courts and judges responsible for interpreting and applying the law.
An independent judiciary is a key safeguard against authoritarianism.
The permanent professional administration that helps implement government policy.
The civil service is expected to remain politically neutral.
All the people who are allowed to vote in an election.
The electorate was deeply divided over the issue of immigration.
The right to vote in political elections.
Universal suffrage was achieved gradually in many democracies.
The legal right to vote; in politics, it is often used as a synonym for suffrage.
Expanding the franchise changed the balance of political power.
A geographical area whose voters elect a representative.
The MP returned to her constituency after the parliamentary debate.
The percentage or number of voters who actually vote in an election.
Low turnout can raise questions about democratic engagement.
The process or principle by which elected officials speak and act for citizens.
Many critics argue that young people lack political representation.
The authority given to a politician or government by voters to carry out a programme.
The prime minister claimed she had a clear mandate for reform.
A vote in which citizens are asked to accept or reject a specific proposal.
The Brexit referendum reshaped British politics.
Basic freedoms protected by law, such as freedom of speech, religion and assembly.
Emergency laws can sometimes threaten civil liberties.
Fundamental rights believed to belong to every person, regardless of nationality, status or identity.
Human rights organisations condemned the arrests of opposition leaders.
responsabilité ; obligation de rendre des comptes
The obligation of leaders and institutions to explain their decisions and accept responsibility for them.
Investigative journalism can increase political accountability.
Openness in decision-making, allowing citizens to see how and why decisions are made.
Transparency is crucial to rebuild trust in public institutions.
Dishonest or illegal behaviour by people in power, often involving bribery or personal gain.
Corruption scandals can destroy public trust in government.
The control or suppression of information, speech, books or media by authorities.
Censorship often increases when governments fear dissent.
Public disagreement with official policies, dominant opinions or political authorities.
Authoritarian regimes often try to silence dissent.
Strong action taken by authorities to stop or punish behaviour they oppose.
The crackdown on protesters was widely criticised abroad.
The gradual weakening of democratic institutions, rights and norms.
Attacks on courts and journalists are often seen as signs of democratic backsliding.
A political system with elections but weak civil liberties, limited checks and balances, and pressure on independent institutions.
An illiberal democracy may hold elections while restricting media freedom.
The right to express opinions and ideas without unjustified government restriction.
Freedom of speech is often at the centre of political debates in liberal democracies.
The right of journalists and media organisations to report news and criticise power without censorship.
Freedom of the press is essential for exposing corruption.
To officially control and manage a country, region or institution.
It is easier to win an election than to govern effectively.
To control a country or people, especially with official or political authority.
The king ruled for more than thirty years.
To possess political authority or control over institutions.
The party has held power for more than a decade.
To begin governing after an election, revolution or political change.
The new coalition came to power after months of political deadlock.
To leave an important position voluntarily, especially in politics.
The minister was forced to step down after the scandal.
To weaken something gradually, especially trust, authority, democracy or institutions.
Disinformation can undermine trust in democratic institutions.
To defend or maintain a law, principle or right.
Courts are expected to uphold constitutional rights.
To make sure that laws, rules or decisions are obeyed.
A law is meaningless if the state cannot enforce it.
To limit or control something that is considered harmful or excessive.
New rules were introduced to curb corruption.
To officially give people legal or political rights.
The reform granted voting rights to millions of citizens.
A formal process in which people vote to choose a political leader or representative.
The next general election is expected to focus on the economy and immigration.
An election in which voters choose representatives for the national legislature, and often indirectly determine the next government.
The party hopes to win a majority in the next general election.
An election in which voters choose a president.
The presidential election has deeply divided the country.
Elections held halfway through a president’s term, especially in the United States.
Midterm elections often become a referendum on the president’s record.
An election in which voters or party members choose a party’s candidate for a later election.
The primary election revealed deep divisions within the party.
An election held to fill a political seat that has become vacant between general elections.
The by-election was seen as a test for the government.
The process of voting, or the paper or electronic form used to vote.
Voters will decide the issue by secret ballot.
The paper on which voters mark their choice in an election.
The candidate’s name was printed at the top of the ballot paper.
A place where people go to vote in an election.
Polling stations opened at 7 a.m. across the country.
Surveys that measure public opinion, especially before an election.
The polls suggest that the race is too close to call.
A survey that asks people what they think about a political issue, party or candidate.
An opinion poll showed growing support for the opposition.
A survey of voters taken just after they have voted, used to predict election results.
The exit poll predicted a landslide victory for the opposition.
A person who has the right to vote or who votes in an election.
Young voters played a decisive role in the election.
The process of officially recording people so that they are allowed to vote.
Campaigners encouraged voter registration among students.
électeur inscrit ou autorisé à voter
A person who meets the legal requirements to vote.
Millions of eligible voters stayed home on election day.
A geographical area whose voters elect a representative.
The candidate has represented the same constituency for twenty years.
An area that elects one or more representatives.
The new map changed the boundaries of several electoral districts.
A person who competes in an election or applies for a position.
The candidate promised to restore trust in politics.
The person currently holding a political office.
The incumbent president faces a strong challenge from the opposition.
A candidate who runs for a lower office alongside another candidate, especially a vice-presidential candidate.
The presidential candidate announced her running mate in July.
The candidate who seems most likely to win an election or nomination.
The former governor quickly became the front-runner.
A candidate or party expected to lose, but who may still surprise observers.
The underdog gained momentum after a strong debate performance.
A public document in which a political party presents its ideas, promises and programme.
The party manifesto focused on housing, healthcare and climate policy.
The set of policies and principles supported by a political party or candidate.
The candidate ran on a platform of tax cuts and stricter border controls.
A serious public promise, especially during an election campaign.
The prime minister broke a key campaign pledge on taxes.
A planned series of activities designed to win an election or influence public opinion.
The campaign focused heavily on the cost-of-living crisis.
The series of events, visits and speeches made by candidates while campaigning.
The candidate spent three weeks on the campaign trail.
A public meeting where supporters gather to hear a candidate speak.
Thousands attended the campaign rally in Manchester.
The practice of contacting voters directly to persuade them or gather information about their voting intentions.
Volunteers spent the weekend canvassing in marginal areas.
Campaigning by visiting voters at home and speaking to them directly.
Door-knocking remains an important part of local campaigns.
A campaign activity in which volunteers call voters to persuade them or remind them to vote.
Phone banking helped the campaign contact thousands of undecided voters.
A printed sheet used to give political information or persuade voters.
The leaflet summarised the candidate’s main proposals.
A political advertisement designed to influence voters.
The political ad accused the opposition of being weak on crime.
A strategy that attacks an opponent rather than promoting one’s own ideas.
Negative campaigning may discourage voters from taking part.
A political advertisement that criticises or attacks an opponent.
The attack ad damaged the candidate’s reputation.
A favourable or strategic interpretation of events designed to influence public opinion.
The minister’s team tried to put a positive spin on the poor results.
A political adviser who manages public image and media messaging.
The spin doctor helped reshape the candidate’s image after the scandal.
A short memorable phrase used in a campaign.
The slogan was simple, emotional and easy to remember.
A short phrase from a speech or interview that is memorable and widely repeated.
The debate produced several powerful soundbites.
A formal public discussion between candidates or political opponents.
The televised debate attracted millions of viewers.
The percentage of people who say they approve of a leader or government.
The president’s approval rating fell after the crisis.
The approval or backing that a person, policy or party receives from citizens.
The reform lost public support after weeks of protests.
A voter who has not firmly decided which party or candidate to support.
Swing voters may decide the outcome in key states.
A voter who has not yet chosen a candidate or party.
Both candidates tried to appeal to undecided voters.
A voter who does not always vote for the same party.
Floating voters are crucial in close elections.
A parliamentary seat that is won by a small margin and could easily change hands.
The party targeted marginal seats in the north of England.
A parliamentary seat that one party is very likely to keep.
The minister represents a safe seat.
A state where the result is uncertain and which may decide an election, especially in the United States.
Pennsylvania was one of the most important battleground states.
A US state where both major parties have a realistic chance of winning.
Candidates spend huge sums of money in swing states.
An election victory by a very large margin.
The opposition won a landslide victory.
An election victory by a small margin.
The president secured a narrow victory in the second round.
A parliament in which no party has an overall majority.
The election resulted in a hung parliament.
A government formed by two or more political parties.
A coalition government was formed after weeks of negotiations.
The number by which one candidate or party has more votes or seats than another; also more than half.
The ruling party lost its majority in parliament.
A government that does not control more than half the seats in parliament.
The prime minister tried to govern with a minority government.
An electoral system in which parties receive seats roughly in proportion to their share of the vote.
Proportional representation often benefits smaller parties.
scrutin uninominal majoritaire à un tour
An electoral system in which the candidate with the most votes in a constituency wins, even without an absolute majority.
The UK uses first-past-the-post for general elections.
A second election held when no candidate wins enough votes in the first round.
The two leading candidates faced each other in a run-off election.
The system used in US presidential elections in which states appoint electors who formally choose the president.
A candidate can win the popular vote but lose the Electoral College.
The total number of votes cast by citizens, as opposed to votes in an electoral college or parliament.
She won the popular vote but lost the presidency.
suppression du vote ; entraves au vote
Actions or rules that make it harder for certain groups of people to vote.
Civil rights groups denounced the law as voter suppression.
The manipulation of electoral district boundaries to benefit a party or group.
Gerrymandering can distort representation in parliament or congress.
Illegal manipulation of an election result.
The opposition accused the government of vote rigging.
Illegal interference with the process or results of an election.
International observers reported signs of electoral fraud.
The orderly handover of political authority from one leader or government to another after an election.
A peaceful transfer of power is a core feature of democracy.
An organised group of people who share political ideas and try to win power through elections.
Political parties compete to win elections and shape public policy.
The way political parties are organised and compete within a country.
Britain’s party system has become more fragmented in recent years.
A political system dominated by two major parties.
The United States is often described as a two-party system.
A political system in which several parties have a realistic chance of influencing government.
A multi-party system can make coalition governments more likely.
The political party currently in power.
The ruling party defended its economic record.
A political party that is not in government and criticises or challenges the ruling party.
Opposition parties accused the government of ignoring the crisis.
An alliance between political parties or groups, often formed to govern together.
The election result forced the parties to form a coalition.
Ordinary members or supporters of a political movement, rather than its leaders.
Grassroots activists helped the campaign gain momentum.
The group of people who officially belong to a political party.
Party membership has declined in many Western democracies.
chef de parti ; dirigeant de parti
The person who leads a political party.

Ordinary members of a party or organisation, not its leaders.

A large formal meeting where a political party discusses policies and strategy.
The prime minister delivered a major speech at the party conference.
A public document in which a political party presents its ideas, promises and programme.
The manifesto included pledges on taxation, housing and climate policy.
The official set of policies and principles supported by a party.
The party platform moved further to the right on immigration.
A plan or course of action adopted by a government, party or institution.
The party’s housing policy was popular among young voters.
A set of political beliefs or principles about how society should be organised.
Political ideology shapes how parties view the role of the state.
Supporting social equality, public services and a more active role for the state in the economy.
Left-wing parties usually support stronger welfare policies.
Supporting tradition, free markets, national identity or limited state intervention, depending on context.
Right-wing parties often call for lower taxes and stricter law enforcement.
Moderately left-wing; usually supportive of welfare and equality but within a market economy.
The centre-left party promised to protect public services.
Moderately right-wing; usually supportive of free markets, fiscal discipline and gradual reform.
The centre-right candidate appealed to business leaders.
The range of political positions from left to right, including moderate and extreme views.
The issue divides voters across the political spectrum.
Having political views that are not extreme.
Moderate voters may reject radical proposals.
A person or party with moderate political views between the left and the right.
The centrist party tried to attract voters tired of polarisation.
Supporting deep or fundamental political change.
Radical movements often emerge during periods of crisis.
A person or group with political views far outside the mainstream, often rejecting democratic norms.
Extremist groups exploited anger and economic insecurity.
Considered normal, dominant or widely accepted in society or politics.
The idea has moved from the margins into mainstream politics.
A small party with views outside the political mainstream.
The fringe party gained attention through social media.
A political style that claims to defend ordinary people against corrupt elites.
Populism often presents politics as a battle between the people and the elite.
A politician, party or movement that uses populist language or ideas.
The populist leader promised to speak for ordinary citizens.
A political belief that emphasises national identity, sovereignty and loyalty to the nation.

Love of and loyalty to one’s country, usually without necessarily rejecting others.
The speech appealed to patriotism rather than hostility to foreigners.
A political ideology that values tradition, stability, gradual change and often limited government.
Conservatism often stresses order, family and national continuity.
A political ideology that emphasises individual rights, freedoms, democracy and the rule of law.
Liberalism places strong emphasis on civil liberties.
A political ideology that supports greater economic equality and public or collective control of key resources.
Socialism has historically called for stronger workers’ rights.
A moderate left-wing ideology that supports democracy, welfare policies and a regulated market economy.
Social democracy seeks to combine capitalism with social protection.
A political ideology that emphasises individual freedom, limited government and free markets.
Libertarianism is sceptical of state intervention.
An ideology favouring free markets, deregulation, privatisation and a reduced role for the state in the economy.
Critics blame neoliberalism for rising inequality.
A political and social movement that seeks to protect the environment.
Environmentalism has become a major force in European politics.
Political ideas focused on environmental protection, sustainability and climate action.
Green politics has pushed climate change higher on the political agenda.
A movement and ideology supporting equal rights and opportunities for women and men.
Feminism has reshaped debates on work, family and political representation.
Political positions based on the interests or experiences of particular identity groups.
Critics and supporters disagree sharply over the role of identity politics.
Political conflicts over values, identity, education, religion, gender or national history.

Supporting social reform, equality and change, especially on social issues.
Progressive voters demanded stronger action on climate change.
Opposed to social or political change and wishing to return to a previous order.

Taking a strict, uncompromising political position.

Supporting strong military action or a tough foreign policy.
Hawkish lawmakers called for tougher sanctions.
Supporting diplomacy, compromise or a softer foreign policy.

Focused on practical results rather than ideology.

The process by which political opinions become more divided and extreme.

Strong loyalty to a political party, sometimes at the expense of fairness or compromise.

A strong loyalty to one’s political group, often leading to hostility towards opponents.

Cooperation between two major political parties.

Support from members of different political parties.
The pressure on elected representatives to vote according to their party’s position.

The official position of a political party on an issue.
Several lawmakers refused to follow the party line.
député de base ; parlementaire sans fonction ministérielle
An ordinary MP who is not a minister or party leader, especially in the UK.

The act of leaving one political party to join another or become independent.

A smaller group within a party that has its own views or interests.

A serious division within a party, movement or group.
The leadership contest exposed a deep split within the party.
A major and lasting change in the support base of parties or political groups.

séduire les électeurs ; parler aux électeurs
The party tried to appeal to younger voters.
aliéner ; se mettre à dos les électeurs
To make voters feel rejected, ignored or hostile.

élargir son électorat ; élargir son audience
To become attractive to a wider range of voters.
The candidate tried to broaden her appeal beyond the party base.
A political system in which citizens choose their leaders through free elections and enjoy political rights and freedoms.
Democracy depends on elections, rights, institutions and public trust.
A democracy that protects individual rights, minority rights, the rule of law and checks on government power.
A liberal democracy protects citizens even when they disagree with the government.
A democracy in which political power is limited by a constitution and legal safeguards.
In a constitutional democracy, leaders cannot simply ignore the law.
A political system with regular elections, though rights and institutions may be weaker than in a liberal democracy.
An electoral democracy may hold elections while still restricting civil liberties.
A model of democracy in which the majority has strong power to make decisions.

Principles such as freedom, equality, fairness, tolerance and respect for the rule of law.

The existence of different political parties, opinions and groups in society.

Elections in which voters can choose freely and results are counted honestly.

The right of all adult citizens to vote, without discrimination based on wealth, gender, race or status.

Active involvement in public life and community or political issues.

Organisations and groups outside the state and the market, such as charities, unions, NGOs and associations.

The space where citizens discuss public issues, through media, debate and civic life.

Open discussion about political or social issues.
A democracy needs informed public debate.
État de droit ; primauté du droit
The principle that everyone, including leaders and governments, must obey the law.

A court system that is free from political pressure and can apply the law fairly.

Legal protections designed to prevent abuses of power.

A system in which different branches of government can limit one another’s power.

Openness in decision-making, allowing citizens to understand how decisions are made.

Media that can report, investigate and criticise power without censorship.

A person who reveals secret or illegal activity within an organisation.

The right to express opinions and ideas without unjustified government restriction.

The right to join groups, parties, unions or organisations.

Rights that allow people to participate in political life, such as voting or standing for office.

défendre les droits ; faire respecter les droits
To defend, respect or maintain legal and political rights.

To protect freedoms from threats or abuses.

The accumulation of political authority in the hands of one person or institution.

To reduce the independence, authority or effectiveness of institutions.

To silence or restrict journalists and media organisations.

To prevent people from expressing disagreement with the authorities.

The control or suppression of information, speech or media by authorities.
Strong action by authorities to stop or punish behaviour they oppose.

To protect democratic institutions, rights and norms.
